narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, family background, and study plans to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 24 (well under the 36.70 average), Year 1 grade decile of 4 and Year 3 decile of 2 (both lower than school averages around 5-6), lower-middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans—and predicted a 50.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try. This borderline-high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. Among all these factors, your family income had the strongest influence, actually pushing the prediction toward success because lower-middle income students in the data often showed determination that led to passing. Next most influential was your LSAT score, which significantly pulled the prediction toward failure since your 24 is substantially below what's typical for bar passers. Your Year 3 grade decile ranked third in impact, also nudging toward failure as your position in the lower half of your class signals challenges in handling rigorous coursework compared to peers. While full-time status is common and neutral here, your overall academic metrics stood out as weaker than most applicants who get approved. This doesn't reflect your full potential as an Asian male with drive, but highlights how these metrics compare to historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward 35+, strengthening your undergraduate record through post-bac courses or a master's, and gathering strong recommendation letters emphasizing your resilience from a lower-middle income background. Many students in similar situations improve these areas and succeed on appeals or at other programs. With targeted effort, you can shift your profile toward the stronger averages that predict bar success.
